title: Jim Gets A Pet

jokes.text: Latka's system for betting on horses is based on the temperature of the horse
jokes.setup: Everyone asks about Latka's winning horse betting system, expecting something logical
jokes.punchline: He bets based on the horse's body temperature, which is completely absurd
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Latka - the logic is so bizarre yet delivered matter-of-factly. The misdirection from air temperature to horse temperature is well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I take horse's temperature. If horse is warm, I bet on horse. If horse is cold, I don't bet.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's it? That's your system?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Is very effective. Warm horse always run fast because blood is moving. Cold horse, blood is slow.

jokes.text: Latka won $12 but lost $36, then claims he can do that well 'every time'
jokes.setup: Latka proudly explains his winnings
jokes.punchline: His 'system' consistently loses money but he treats this as a reliable success
jokes.explanation: Great character consistency - Latka's warped logic presented as confidence. The mathematical absurdity is presented without fanfare.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I tell you, my system, it never fails. Every day I go to the track, I use my special method, and every day I come home with empty pockets.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You know why? Because it works! My system is so reliable, I know for certain I will lose money. And I am never wrong!

jokes.text: 'I'll show you mine if you show me yours' exchange about vices
jokes.setup: Discussion about having vices
jokes.punchline: Sexual innuendo phrase applied to innocent topic
jokes.explanation: Predictable innuendo joke. Gets a mild reaction but relies on familiar comedic territory.

jokes.text: Jim looking for replacement for 'uh, uh, what do you call it?' 'Drugs?' 'No, thanks.'
jokes.setup: Jim searching for word to describe what gambling replaces in his life
jokes.punchline: When offered 'drugs' he politely declines as if being offered them now
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim Ignatowski moment - the polite decline treats it as a current offer rather than past reference

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What word describes what gambling replaces in my life?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Drugs.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No thank you, I'm all set.

jokes.text: Jim wants to bet on a man getting a vodka gimlet from a coffee machine
jokes.setup: Jim explains how gambling makes ordinary moments thrilling
jokes.punchline: He bets on someone getting an impossible drink from a basic coffee machine
jokes.explanation: Shows Jim's gambling addiction through absurd optimism. The specificity of 'vodka gimlet' from a basic machine is perfectly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know what gambling does? It makes ordinary moments thrilling. Like, I bet Bobby five bucks he couldn't get a decent espresso out of that coffee machine.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I can do that.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, you can't. Nobody can. That machine hasn't made anything decent since 1987.

jokes.text: 'Give him odds.' 'A hundred to one.' 'I can't pass that by.'
jokes.setup: Jim wants to bet on impossible drink choice
jokes.punchline: Despite astronomical odds, Jim can't resist the bet
jokes.explanation: Shows Jim's gambling addiction developing - can't resist any odds

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'll bet you a hundred bucks I can drink a martini made with motor oil, tabasco sauce, and a raw egg.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You're crazy. Nobody could drink that.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I can do it.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Jim, those are impossible odds. Don't take that bet.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm doing it anyway.

jokes.text: Guy orders vodka gimlet but it comes out as coffee, Jim still treats it as a thrill
jokes.setup: The impossible bet plays out exactly as expected
jokes.punchline: Jim thanks Bobby for giving him 'that thrill' despite the obvious outcome
jokes.explanation: Good payoff showing Jim's addiction has made him delusional about what constitutes excitement. His gratitude is the key comedic element.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Thanks for the bet, Bobby. You really gave me that thrill.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: What thrill? You knew you were gonna win the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I know, but you gave me the thrill of hope.

jokes.text: Hey, Jim, I didn't mean to take your last dollar. - Then give it back. - Okay. - Thanks. - You're welcome.
jokes.setup: Bobby feels bad about taking Jim's money
jokes.punchline: Jim's simple logic cuts through the guilt with immediate solution
jokes.explanation: Jim's simple logic cuts through social awkwardness. Good character moment with economic dialogue.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Jim, I feel terrible about taking your money. I don't know how I can ever make this up to you.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's simple. Give it back.

jokes.text: Louie's loan has 85% interest and allows him to sell Jim's body for medical experiments while alive
jokes.setup: Louie offers Jim a gambling loan with a contract
jokes.punchline: The fine print allows selling Jim's living body to science
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy that perfectly captures Louie's predatory nature. The escalation from high interest to body harvesting is shocking and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You want the loan or not? Sign here.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, what's this clause about selling my living body to science?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, that. Standard collateral. You don't pay up, I sell you to science. While you're still using it.

jokes.text: 'You mean after I'm dead.' 'No.'
jokes.setup: Jim trying to understand the body selling clause
jokes.punchline: Louie clarifies it's while Jim is still alive
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation with perfect economy - single word 'No' makes it much worse

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, you're saying I can sell my body?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's right.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: My whole body?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, while you're still alive, yes.

jokes.text: Elaine holds playing cards while lecturing about gambling
jokes.setup: Elaine is anti-gambling
jokes.punchline: She's literally holding cards while giving anti-gambling sermon
jokes.explanation: Standard hypocrisy joke. The visual irony is clear but not particularly clever or surprising.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Gambling is a disease. It destroys lives, ruins families, tears apart the fabric of society.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Elaine, you're holding cards.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I know. I'm just about to deal.

jokes.text: 'She's got kings and queens?' 'I fold.'
jokes.setup: Alex mentions Elaine has cards with kings and queens
jokes.punchline: Tony thinks it's about poker hands and immediately folds
jokes.explanation: Quick misdirection joke about misinterpreting card description

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Kings and queens? I'm out. That's a straight flush waiting to happen.

jokes.text: Tony folds when he hears the cards have kings and queens, Alex says he's back in when told there's no money
jokes.setup: Card game discussion about what's in the hands
jokes.punchline: Tony and Alex have opposite reactions to high cards vs. no stakes
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing different gambling philosophies. Quick and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I got nothing. Not a pair, not a straight, nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I got three aces.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Three aces? What are we playing for?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Nothing. No stakes.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Nothing? Then what do I care? I'm happy!

jokes.text: Elaine's elaborate scenario of Jim's gambling downfall with loan sharks
jokes.setup: Elaine wants to warn Jim about gambling dangers
jokes.punchline: Her specific, dramatic scenario becomes increasingly absurd
jokes.explanation: The scenario setup is familiar but Elaine's intensity makes it work. A bit lengthy for the payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jim, I have to warn you about gambling. It starts innocently enough, but then you lose a little money, then a little more, and before you know it, you've lost your house, your car, your family...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're living under a bridge, wearing a barrel, selling pencils door-to-door just to buy a cup of coffee...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And then one day you wake up and you realize you've become a ventriloquist's dummy, and the ventriloquist doesn't even like you that much!

jokes.text: Jim's naive responses to the loan shark scenario - he'll call the cops
jokes.setup: Elaine describes dangerous criminals
jokes.punchline: Jim thinks normal law enforcement will solve everything
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim innocence. His complete lack of street smarts is endearing and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: These are dangerous criminals we're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, what do you want me to do about it?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I don't know, call the police!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: The police? They'll take care of it. They always do.

jokes.text: The cop scenario reveals Jim's drug history and traffic violations, he corrects 'Five! Five lights!'
jokes.setup: Police are supposed to help Jim
jokes.punchline: They have a file on his past crimes, Jim defensively corrects the count
jokes.explanation: Excellent character revelation and his indignant correction of the red light count is perfectly Jim. Shows his past while maintaining his childlike precision about details.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: We have a file on your past crimes.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Past crimes? Plural? It was just the one time!

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: We have documentation of at least five incidents.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Five? No, no—it was three. Three at most.

jokes.text: In the scenario, strange men took Jim's mama because he owes money
jokes.setup: Jim thinks he can hide with his parents
jokes.punchline: The criminals have already kidnapped his mother
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the scenario's stakes. The casual mention of mama being taken is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: My parents will protect me. I can hide at their place.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jim, the criminals have already kidnapped your mother.

jokes.text: Priest says 'I don't do priests' when Jim wants to confess
jokes.setup: Jim's last resort is confession
jokes.punchline: Even the priest won't help because of who Jim is
jokes.explanation: Funny escalation showing Jim is so problematic even clergy won't deal with him. Economical and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I've tried everything else. My last resort is confession.

dialogue.speaker: Priest
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, but I can't help you.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why not? I thought confession was for everyone.

dialogue.speaker: Priest
dialogue.line: Not for you. I know who you are.

jokes.text: Jim bet on a 300-to-1 shot named 'On Dasher' because he identifies with long shots
jokes.setup: Explaining his incredible bet
jokes.punchline: His reason is philosophical identification with underdogs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim philosophy - his reason for the bet is both touching and ridiculous. Great character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I bet on a horse named Mavis today.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why would I do that? Because that horse is an underdog, and I identify with underdogs. We're the same, that horse and me.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We both show up, we both try our best, and we both usually lose.

jokes.text: Jim spent all $10,000 and says he didn't go into any of the 84 bars between track and garage
jokes.setup: Jim spent all his winnings somehow
jokes.punchline: He knows exactly how many bars he passed but didn't drink the money away
jokes.explanation: Great Jim detail - his precise count of bars shows his unique mind. Sets up the real revelation nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: He knows exactly how many bars he passed but didn't drink the money away

jokes.text: Jim bought the horse and they threw in the saddle
jokes.setup: Jim spent $10,000 on something mysterious
jokes.punchline: He bought the actual racehorse, saddle included as if it's a car dealership bonus
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist revelation. The casual mention of the saddle as a throw-in is perfectly ridiculous. Pure Jim logic and completely unexpected.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I bought a racehorse.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: They threw in the saddle.

jokes.text: Everyone's reaction to seeing a racehorse in the garage
jokes.setup: Jim mentions buying a horse
jokes.punchline: There's actually a full-sized racehorse standing in their workplace
jokes.explanation: Major visual gag payoff. The sight of a racehorse in a taxi garage would be inherently hilarious and shocking.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I bought a horse.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You bought a horse? What are you, crazy?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, it's a great investment.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Jim, there's a horse standing in the middle of the garage.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Jim's reason for buying the horse - it won money for him so he returned the favor
jokes.setup: Jim explains his irrational purchase
jokes.punchline: He felt obligated to reciprocate the horse's favor
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character moment showing Jim's unique moral compass. His logic is absurd but emotionally coherent for him.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: He felt obligated to reciprocate the horse's favor

jokes.text: The horse cost exactly what Jim had - 'How much you got?' 'And would you believe it? It was the same.'
jokes.setup: Jim asks how much the horse costs
jokes.punchline: The seller asks what he has, and it's exactly that amount by 'coincidence'
jokes.explanation: Shows Jim's naivety about being taken advantage of. His amazement at the 'coincidence' is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How much does the horse cost?

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: What have you got?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I've got $500.

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: That's exactly what the horse costs.

jokes.text: Jim is giving the horse his freedom, Alex says 'He's a 300-to-1 shot. We can outrun him.'
jokes.setup: Everyone panics about catching a racehorse
jokes.punchline: Alex realizes this particular horse is slow enough to catch on foot
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that uses the horse's poor odds as practical information. Perfect timing and logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We gotta catch that horse before it gets away!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. Guys, relax. It's okay.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What do you mean relax? It's a racehorse!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, but... I've seen this horse race. We can walk after it.

jokes.text: Jim doesn't want to call the horse by his 'slave name' On Dasher, renames him Gary
jokes.setup: Alex refers to the horse by his racing name
jokes.punchline: Jim has applied liberation philosophy to a racehorse and given him a mundane human name
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Jim's 60s counterculture background and his tendency to overthink liberation. 'Gary' as a horse name is beautifully mundane.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I've liberated that horse from the tyranny of his racing name. He's no longer 'Thunderbolt' or whatever corporate designation they branded him with.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Now he goes by his true name: Frank.

jokes.text: 'What's his new name?' 'Gary.'
jokes.setup: Alex asking what Jim renamed the freed horse
jokes.punchline: The most mundane human name possible for a racehorse
jokes.explanation: Perfect anticlimax - the most ordinary name for extraordinary situation

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What did you name the horse you freed?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I named him Fred.

jokes.text: Louie's insult about the ugly, smelly, dirty creature and telling him to take his horse with him
jokes.setup: Louie complaining about the situation
jokes.punchline: Deliberately ambiguous whether he's describing Jim or the horse first
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie meanness with a well-constructed misdirection. The ambiguity is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what I got? A broken-down cab, a driver who can't find his way out of the garage, and a horse that's got more sense than both of us combined!

jokes.text: Louie's Easter seals follow-up to the glue joke
jokes.setup: Someone objects to Louie's suggestion about making glue from the horse
jokes.punchline: Louie makes it 'nice' by using the glue for charity
jokes.explanation: Good Louie comeback that makes his cruelty even worse by adding false charity. The specificity of Easter seals is nice.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'nice'? I'll make it nice. We'll use the glue for charity.

jokes.text: I, uh... like your place, uh, Jim. - It's... it's you, Jim.
jokes.setup: Bobby tries to compliment Jim's clearly awful apartment
jokes.punchline: The struggle to find something positive to say
jokes.explanation: Standard polite awkwardness about bad living conditions. Functional but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Jim, I gotta say, your apartment... it's really something.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah? You like it?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Well, it's got... walls. Definitely walls.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's true.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: And a floor. That's important in an apartment.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: The ceiling is... also here. So you've got that going for you.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Thanks, Bobby.

jokes.text: Jim's apartment was 'a fixer-upper' when he got it - but it's a condemned building
jokes.setup: Compliment about Jim's apartment
jokes.punchline: Jim describes a condemned building as a 'fixer-upper'
jokes.explanation: Perfect understatement joke. Jim's optimistic real estate terminology for uninhabitable housing is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's not condemned, it's a fixer-upper.

jokes.text: 'When I first got it, it was a fixer-upper.'
jokes.setup: Jim's apartment is clearly a disaster
jokes.punchline: Jim implies it used to be worse
jokes.explanation: Good ironic understatement showing Jim's obliviousness to how bad things are

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know it looks bad, but you should've seen it last week.

jokes.text: Jim gets electricity from an extension cord to another apartment and hangs laundry on it so they don't notice
jokes.setup: Question about his electricity source
jokes.punchline: Elaborate scheme involving theft disguised as laundry line
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Jim ingenuity. The laundry camouflage is both clever and completely in character for his unconventional problem-solving.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, I've got this elaborate scheme. I run a wire from the laundromat next door, disguise it as a clothesline, and nobody suspects a thing.

jokes.text: Jim likes living in condemned building because 'nothing can happen to it - what are they going to do, save it?'
jokes.setup: Suggestion he could afford better housing
jokes.punchline: His warped logic about the benefits of rock-bottom living
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jim philosophy. His reverse psychology about real estate is both funny and oddly wise. Great economy and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what the great thing about living in a terrible apartment is? There's nowhere to go but up. Literally. My ceiling leaks, so every time it rains, I get a little shower. Free water bill. My neighbors are so loud, I don't need a stereo. And the roaches? They're like tiny roommates who pay rent in entertainment value.

jokes.text: Jim walks Gary in the park and 'those poodle people have stopped acting like they own the place'
jokes.setup: Question about reactions to walking a horse
jokes.punchline: Focus is on how it solved a completely different social problem
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection. Instead of addressing the obvious strangeness, Jim focuses on the social dynamics. Very Jim perspective.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know what the amazing thing was? Nobody mugged me.

jokes.text: Vet wants to examine Jim while looking at the horse
jokes.setup: Horse needs medical examination
jokes.punchline: Vet thinks Jim needs examination too
jokes.explanation: Solid joke about Jim's own questionable health/hygiene. Quick and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Vet
dialogue.line: I've examined your horse. Now I think you need an examination too.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why? What's wrong with me?

dialogue.speaker: Vet
dialogue.line: Anyone who owns a horse like this and brings it to me must be crazy.

jokes.text: Jim uses his table as a nutcracker
jokes.setup: Bobby asks for a nutcracker
jokes.punchline: Jim's 'table' is actually just for cracking nuts
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag showing Jim's unconventional household items. Reveals his poverty through creative repurposing.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Do you have a nutcracker?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, but I have a table.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: A table? What good is a table?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, it's actually for cracking nuts.

jokes.text: Jim demonstrates violent nut-cracking technique
jokes.setup: Bobby tries to crack nuts Jim's way
jokes.punchline: Jim shows proper technique which is presumably excessive force
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy moment. Shows Jim's alternative lifestyle through simple actions.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You gotta crack 'em like this. You take the nut, you put it in the vice, you tighten the vice real good, then you hit it with the hammer.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That seems like a lot of work.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Work? That's the only way to do it right.

jokes.text: It's 'not true what they say about horses sleeping standing up'
jokes.setup: Common knowledge about horses
jokes.punchline: Sets up revelation that Gary is dead, not sleeping
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup for the major revelation. The misdirection is perfectly planted and pays off soon after.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what they say about horses? If a horse is lying down and won't get up, it's probably dead.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So I go to check on Gary this morning...

jokes.text: Horse has been sleeping since 'the day before yesterday'
jokes.setup: Question about how long the horse has been sleeping
jokes.punchline: Absurdly long time that suggests death, which Jim doesn't recognize
jokes.explanation: Great build-up to the revelation. Jim's innocent delivery of clearly concerning information is perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How long has the horse been sleeping?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Three weeks.

jokes.text: 'I guess winning that race took a lot out of him.'
jokes.setup: Horse clearly dead from exhaustion
jokes.punchline: Jim's massive understatement about fatal exertion
jokes.explanation: Perfect understatement - death described as being tired from winning

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, I think he's a little tired.

jokes.text: Bobby asks 'Which one?' when told not to wake up the guest in the guest room
jokes.setup: Jim warns about waking someone in the guest room
jokes.punchline: Bobby can't tell which is the guest between Jim and the dead horse
jokes.explanation: Good visual joke about the state of both Jim and the horse. Economical and well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Whatever you do, don't wake the person sleeping in the guest room.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Which one is the guest - you or the dead horse?

jokes.text: Tony asks 'The horse hung himself?' when told 'he died'
jokes.setup: Bobby says the horse died
jokes.punchline: Tony jumps to suicide conclusion
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that's economical but not particularly sophisticated. Gets the shock value intended.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: The horse died.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh no! I'm gonna kill myself!

jokes.text: Jim refers to Gary being 'bred and raised to run' in the eulogy
jokes.setup: Eulogy for the horse
jokes.punchline: 'Bred and raised' pun on horse breeding and upbringing
jokes.explanation: Subtle wordplay within a touching moment. The pun works without undermining the emotional weight.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: He was bred and raised right here in this city.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And he turned out to be a fine horse.

jokes.text: Gary thought 'in his heart, he was still a two-year-old'
jokes.setup: Eulogy about the horse's decline
jokes.punchline: The horse's internal self-image versus reality
jokes.explanation: Beautiful metaphor that works as both horse observation and human commentary. Jim's insight is surprisingly profound.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: He was a magnificent creature in his own mind.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But let's face it, he was just a broken-down nag that couldn't pull a cab uphill without stopping for a nap.

jokes.text: Louie asks Jim to do his mother's eulogy, saying he wouldn't need to change the words much
jokes.setup: Louie is moved by Jim's eulogy
jokes.punchline: Implies his mother is comparable to an old, broken-down racehorse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - dark, inappropriate, but also touching in its own way. The economy and character integration are flawless.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That was beautiful, Jim. Really beautiful. You know, that reminds me of my mother.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Your mother?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah. She's just like that old racehorse — still got a little life left in her, but mostly just waiting for someone to put her out to pasture.

tmdb_overview: Jim invests his race-track winnings in a lame horse that he wants to set free--in New York City.
insight: Forty-six jokes in 20 minutes keep absurdist energy high despite middling impact scores.
